coredata.dc:title: Tailoring of silica nanoarchitecture to optimize Cu(2−x)S based image-guided chemodynamic therapy agent
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Fedorenko, Svetlana
Stepanov, Alexey
Bochkova, Olga
Kholin, Kirill
Dovjenko, Alexey
Zairov, Rustem
Nizameev, Irek
Gerasimova, Tatiana
Strelnik, Igor
Voloshina, Alexandra
Sapunova, Anastasia
Gumerova, Syumbelya
Gubaidullin, Aidar
Fedosimova, Svetlana
Evtugyn, Vladimir
Toropchina, Asiya
Karasik, Andrey
Mustafina, Asiya
coredata.dc:description: The present work introduces various synthetic and post-synthetic modes of co-doping of oleate-oleylamine-stabilized Cu(2−x)S cores with red emitting [Ru(dipy)3]2+ into amino-modified silica nanoparticles (SNs) with the aim to combine chemodynamic therapy (CDT) with cellular uptake and imaging functions. Thereto, the ROS generation by the Cu(2−x)S embedded into the SNs was manifested by the spin trap facilitated ESR technique and correlated with such parameters as size of the embedded cores, content and oxidation extent of copper ions. The parameters were varied through the different extent of an oxidative degradation of the embedded Cu(2−x)S cores by their post-treating with histidine, polyethylenimine and citrate-stabilized carbon dots (CDs). The treating by CDs was chosen as the optimal post-synthetic modification of the composite SNs due to combination of CDT with high stability to aggregation. The green or dual green-red emission of the CD-treated composite SNs visualizes their cell internalization and intracellular localization by means of fluorescence and confocal microscopy methods. Correlation of cytotoxicity data of the differently treated composite SNs with their ability to ROS generation and the intracellular localization highlight the enhanced cell internalization and nuclear confinement of the CD-treated composite SNs as the factor enhancing their cytotoxicity in greater extent than the CDT function.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cu(2−x)S nanoparticles
Silica shell
Carbon dots
Chemodynamic therapy
ROS generation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The list of the purchased reagents and common purification techniques are in the ESI
Cellular uptake study of [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs and CDs-Cu(2−x)S@SNs
Synthesis and characterization of oleate-oleylamine-stabilized and silica coated Cu(2−x)S NPs
Post-synthetic surface decoration of Cu(2−x)S@SNs and [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs
Visualization of chemodynamic activities of the composite SNs
Cell viability and cell internalization of the composite SNs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors gratefully acknowledge to Assigned Spectral-Analytical Center of FRC Kazan Scientific Center of RAS for providing necessary facilities to carry out physical-chemical measurements. The authors thanks to Interdisciplinary Center for Analytical Microscopy at KFU for assistance with the confocal microscopy experiments. The work was supported by RFBR and CNR Project No. 20-53-7802. No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).

Authors gratefully acknowledge to Assigned Spectral-Analytical Center of FRC Kazan Scientific Center of RAS for providing necessary facilities to carry out physical-chemical measurements. The authors thanks to Interdisciplinary Center for Analytical Microscopy at KFU for assistance with the confocal microscopy experiments. The work was supported by RFBR and CNR Project No. 20-53-7802 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), size distribution (B), XRD pattern, red vertical bars show the positions of the interference peaks corresponding to the crystalline Digenite, Cu1.765S, syn., Code No. 00-023-0960; (C), Transmittance (T, %) of Cu1.765S in cyclohexane (C = 0.15 g∙L−1) (D).
Schematic presentation of synthesis Cu(2−x)S@SNs, [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs and CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs.
TEM images (A, B) of Cu(2−x)S@SNs (A), [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs (B) and HR-TEM image of [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs (C).

(A) TEM images of Cu(2−x)S@SNs after post-treatment of histidine, (B) [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs after post-treatment of PEI; (C) emission spectra of CDs (1, black line), CDs-Cu(2−x)S@SNs (2, red line) and CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs (3, blue line) (concentration ratio С(SNs):С(CQD) = 1:20); (λex = 465 nm); (D) schematic presentation of the CDs-triggered de-aggregation of [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs.

X-band ESR spectrum of the DMPO-based spin-adducts generated by: (a) [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs in the aqueous solution; (b) [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs treated by histidine; (c, d, e) represent the simulated spectra of the mixture of DMPO-OH∙ and DMPO-X∙ (с), DMPO-OH∙ (d), DMPO-X∙ (e).

(A) Cell viability of M-Hela (1), WI-38 (2) and Chang Liver (3) cell lines plotted vs concentration of [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs; flow cytometry data for: (B) M-HeLa cells, 1 – Control, 2 – incubated by CDs-Cu(2−x)S@SNs (0.063 g L−1); (C) Chang liver cells, 1 – Control, 2, 3 – incubated by [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs at 0.25 g L−1 (2) and 0.35 g L−1 (3).

Fluorescent microscopy images of control Chang Liver cell lines (a, b, c) and Chang Liver, incubated with [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs at 0.25 g L−1 (d, e, f) and at 0.35 g L−1 (g, h, i) (Nikon eclipse Ci, x100 oil).

Confocal microscopy images of control M-HeLa cell lines (A, B, C) and M-HeLa, incubated with of CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs (D, E, F). The monitoring through blue (410–475 nm), green (499–606 nm) and red (613–718 nm in Fig. S9) channels was done under laser excitations at 405, 488 and 458 nm correspondingly.

(a) Apoptotic effects of CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs (0.063 g L−1) on M-HeLa cells visualized by ﬂow cytometry protocol with staining by Annexin V- Alexa Fluor 647. The values are presented as the mean ± SD (n = 3). L – living cells; D – dead cells; Ea – early apoptotic cells; La - late apoptotic cells. (b) Cartoon illustration of the intracellular distribution and CDT effect of CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs.

The Si:Cu:Ru molar ratios of Cu(2−x)S@SNs and [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs calculated from ICP-OES data, diameter nanoparticles (dTEM) from TEM, averaged size (d), electrokinetic potential (ζ), polydispersity indices (PDI) from DLS measurements in various conditions (CSNs:CCDs = 1:20).
Cytotoxicity of Cu(2−x)S@SNs and [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs after post-treatment by histidine, CDs and PEI.

head.ce:title: Tailoring of silica nanoarchitecture to optimize Cu(2−x)S based image-guided chemodynamic therapy agent

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Kazan (Volga region) Federal University, 18 Kremlyovskaya str., 420008 Kazan, Russian Federation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Kazan National Research Technical University named after A.N. Tupolev - KAI, 10, K. Marx str., Kazan 420111, Russian Federation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Kazan National Research Technical University named after A.N. Tupolev - KAI

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Kazan National Research Technical University named after A.N. Tupolev - KAI, 10, K. Marx str., Kazan 420111, Russia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present work introduces various synthetic and post-synthetic modes of co-doping of oleate-oleylamine-stabilized Cu(2−x)S cores with red emitting [Ru(dipy)3]2+ into amino-modified silica nanoparticles (SNs) with the aim to combine chemodynamic therapy (CDT) with cellular uptake and imaging functions. Thereto, the ROS generation by the Cu(2−x)S embedded into the SNs was manifested by the spin trap facilitated ESR technique and correlated with such parameters as size of the embedded cores, content and oxidation extent of copper ions. The parameters were varied through the different extent of an oxidative degradation of the embedded Cu(2−x)S cores by their post-treating with histidine, polyethylenimine and citrate-stabilized carbon dots (CDs). The treating by CDs was chosen as the optimal post-synthetic modification of the composite SNs due to combination of CDT with high stability to aggregation. The green or dual green-red emission of the CD-treated composite SNs visualizes their cell internalization and intracellular localization by means of fluorescence and confocal microscopy methods. Correlation of cytotoxicity data of the differently treated composite SNs with their ability to ROS generation and the intracellular localization highlight the enhanced cell internalization and nuclear confinement of the CD-treated composite SNs as the factor enhancing their cytotoxicity in greater extent than the CDT function.

ce:section.ce:para: Great biomedical potential of copper sulfide nanoparticles Cu(2−x)S NPs has been already well documented, although the published reports are predominantly focused on the photothermal ablation and photodynamic effect as the reasons for the cell treating by Cu(2−x)S NPs [1–3]. However, high redox potential of Cu(I) ions is the well-known reason for the CDT owing to their capacity to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) via Cu-catalyzed Fenton-like reactions in tumor cells [4–6]. Compared to photodynamic therapy (PDT) based on ROS generation under continuous light irradiation, CDT of Cu(I)-based NPs is light-independent, since it derives from the redox activity of transition metal ions, which can be an advantage for deep-tissue therapy of drug-resistant tumors [3,7]. The applicability for CDT has been already documented for both MOF-like nanoarchitectures [2,8–10] and Cu(2−x)S NPs [11,12], although literature data are insufficient for recognition of optimal morphology of Cu(2−x)S NPs for their applicability as CDT agents.

From a viewpoint of biomedical application Cu(2−x)S NPs must at least combine an ability for CDT cell treating with high cellular contrasting effect to reveal their cell internalization. The already documented synthetic procedures demonstrate that similar to iron oxides hydrophilic Cu(2−x)S NPs should be synthesized in two stages with getting hydrophobic oleate-oleylamine-stabilized Cu(2−x)S NPs at the first stage and further hydrophilization at the second one. The two-step synthetic route is advantageous to control both phase and size of Cu(2−x)S NPs, although specificity of Cu(2−x)S NPs should be kept in mind at the second synthetic stage.

Silica shell is permeable to small molecules, including oxygen and water, although its thickness should be of great impact on the diffusion of small molecules to Cu(II)/Cu(I) centers in the silica coated Cu(2−x)S NPs. Thus, the nanoarchitecture of silica coated Cu(2−x)S NPs should be optimized for enough surface exposure of Cu(I) ions in combination with high colloidal stability and efficient cell internalization, which can be visualized through the co-doping of the silica nanoparticles (SNs) by both Cu(2−x)S NPs and luminophores. The present report is aimed at introducing of the optimal synthetic strategy for luminescent Cu(2−x)S-doped SNs fitting to the aforesaid requirements.

In the accordance with the assigned aim the main features of the differently synthesized and/or post-synthetically treated Cu(2−x)S-doped SNs will be correlated with their functional properties, namely, the ability to ROS generation, aggregation and cellular uptake behavior, which, in turn, are the factors affecting a cytotoxicity of nanomaterial. Thus, the cytotoxic effect of both unmodified and surface modified Cu(2−x)S-doped SNs should be correlated with the aforesaid factors.

The introduced synthetic strategies are based on covalent modification by amino-groups and non-covalent adsorption of green emitting citrate-stabilized carbon dots, while the treating of the Cu(2−x)S-doped SNs by histidine and polyehyleneimine (PEI) is aimed to reveal their potential to strip copper ions and/or to be absorbed at the surface of the Cu(2−x)S-doped SNs. The coordinative capacity of the surface exposed copper (I)/(II) ions will be highlighted as the factor affecting both incontrollable aggregation of silica coated Cu(2−x)S NPs and their post-synthetic surface modification. The oxidative leaching of the copper centers from the Cu(2−x)S-doped SNs resulted from their post-synthetic treatment is also discussed in correlation with their nanoarchitecture. The impact of the surface modification modes on the ROS generation by Cu(2−x)S-doped SNs without light irradiation studied by the ESR technique with the use of 5,5- dimethyl-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as spin trap will be also introduced. The functional properties of the composite SNs are correlated with their cellular uptake, intracellular distribution and effect on the cell viability of both cancer and normal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The list of the purchased reagents and common purification techniques are in the ESI
ce:section.ce:para: The previously documented synthetic procedures (SNs, Cu(2−x)S NPs, the stirring-facilitated synthesis of Cu(2−x)S@SNs and [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs) along with the designation of common methods (transmission electron microscopy (TEM), inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES), Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), UV–VIS and ESR spectroscopy, luminescence, dynamic light scattering measurements (DLS), pH measurement, confocal and fluorescence microscopy, flow cytometry and cytotoxicity assays, cell apoptosis analysis) are presented in the ESI.

0.0126 g of the synthesized oleate-coated Cu(2−x)S nanoparticles (8.71 nm) were dispersed in 15.8 mL of cyclohexane using ultrasound water bath for 60 min. Then, the obtained dispersion (15 mL) was added under Ar to the mixture of 8.625 g of Triton X-100, 8.1 mL n-hexanol, 18.75 mL of cyclohexane. The mixture was subjected to ultrasound for 30 min followed by adding of NH3 (28–30%) 0.27 mL, after the ultrasound for 15 min 1.7 mL H2O (for preparing of Cu(2−x)S@SNs) or 1.7 mL of aqueous solution of [Ru(dipy)3Cl2 (C = 0.01 M, for preparing [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs) was added. The mixture was stirred for 15 min (750 rpm) before the adding of TEOS (0.45 mL). Then, the microemulsion was stirred for 16 h (750 rpm) before the adding of APTES (0.0875 mL), followed by further stirring for 3 h. The synthesis was carried out in Ar atmosphere. After the stirring for 3 h the nanoparticles were separated from the mixture by adding of acetone and were then washed with acetone/ethanol mixture (1:1), ethanol by 2X and water by 3X centrifugation/redispersion steps.

The adsorption of Cu(II) onto SNs was performed as follows: aqueous colloids of SNs (2.8 g L−1) were mixed with CuCl2 (5 mM) using a shaker set-up during 1 day. Next, the mixture was left to stay for 2 days after which the SNs were separated by centrifugation (15 m, 15,000 rpm, 4 °C), washed 3 times to remove unbound Cu(II) ions.

The composite SNs (0.5 g L−1) were mixed with DL-histidine (3.3 mM) or PEI (1.0 g L−1) or CDs (10.8 g L−1) using a shaker set-up during 1 day. Afterwards, the treated SNs were separated by centrifugation (15 m, 15,000 rpm, 4 °C) and triply washed.

Cytotoxic effects of samples on human cancer and normal cells were estimated by means of the multifunctional Cytell Cell Imaging system (GE Health Care Life Science, Sweden) using the Cell Viability Bio App which precisely counts the number of cells and evaluates their viability from fluorescence intensity data [13]. DAPI and propidium iodide were purchased from Sigma. The M-HeLa clone 11 human, epithelioid cervical carcinoma, strain of Hela, clone of M-Hela; WI-38 VA-13 cell culture, subline 2RA (human embryonic lung) from the Type Culture Collection of the Institute of Cytology (Russian Academy of Sciences) and Chang liver cell line (Human liver cells) from N. F. Gamaleya Research Center of Epidemiology and Microbiology were used in the experiments. The cells were cultured in a standard Eagle’s nutrient medium manufactured at the Chumakov Institute of Poliomyelitis and Virus Encephalitis (PanEco company) and supplemented with 10% fetal calf serum and 1% nonessential amino acids. The cells were plated into a 96-well plate (Eppendorf) at a concentration of 1×105 cells mL−1, 150 μL of medium per well, and cultured in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C. Twenty four hours after seeding the cells into wells, the compound under study was added at a preset dilution, 150 μL to each well. The dilutions of the compounds were prepared immediately in nutrient media; 5% DMSO that does not induce the inhibition of cells at this concentration was added for better solubility. The experiments were repeated three times. Intact cells cultured in parallel with experimental cells were used as a control.

M-HeLa cells in the amount of 1 × 10 5 cells/well in a final volume of 500 μL were sown in 24-well plates (Eppendorf). After 24-hour incubation, CDs-Cu(2−x)S@SNs-at a concentration of 0.063 g L−1 was added to the wells and incubated for 24 h in a CO2 incubator.

Chang Liver cells in the amount of 1 × 105 cells/well in a final volume of 500 μL were sown in 24-well plates (Eppendorf). After 24-hour incubation, [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs at a concentration of 0.25 g L- 1 and 0.35 g L−1 were added to the wells and incubated for 24 h in a CO2 incubator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake study of [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs and CDs-Cu(2−x)S@SNs
ce:section.ce:para: Cellular uptake of CDs-Cu(2−x)S@SNs and [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs were analyzed by flow cytometry (Guava easy Cyte 8HT, USA). Flow cytometry was used to set up statistics on the uptake of drug by cancer cells. Untreated cells were used as negative control.

M-HeLa cells at 1 × 105 cells/well in a final volume of 2 mL were seeded into 6-well coverslip plates at the bottom of each well. After 24 h of incubation, solutions of the studied CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs (C = 0.063 g L−1) were added to the wells and cultured for 24 h in a CO2 incubator. Then, M-HeLa cells were fixed and stained with DAPI (blue, cell nucleus staining).

Chang Liver cells in an amount of 1 × 105 cells/well in a final volume of 2 mL were seeded in 6-well plates with coverslips at the bottom of each well. After 24 h of incubation, [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs at a concentration 0.25 g L−1 and 0.35 g L−1 were added to the wells and incubated for 24 h in a CO2 incubator. Then, after treatment with solution [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs, Chang Liver cells were stained with DAPI.
The studies were carried out using a Nikon Eclipse Ci-S fluorescence microscope (Nikon, Japan) at 1000x magnification.

M-HeLa cells at 1 × 106 cells/ well in a ﬁnal volume of 2 mL were seeded into 6-well plates. The cells were harvested at 2000 rpm for 5 min and, then, washed twice with ice-cold PBS, followed by resuspension in binding buffer 100 μL. Next, the CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs were incubated with 0.35 μL of annexin V-Alexa Fluor 647 and 0.1 μL of propidium iodide for 40 min at room temperature in the dark. Finally, the cells were analyzed by ﬂow cytometry (Guava easy Cyte, MERCK, USA). The experiments were repeated three times.

The control M-Hela cell lines and cells, incubated with CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs were visualized by CLMS on an inverted Carl Zeiss LSM 780 confocal laser-scanning microscope (Carl Zeiss, Jena, Germany).

M-Hela cells in an amount of 1 × 105 cells/well in a final volume of 2 mL were seeded in 6-well plates with coverslips at the bottom of each well. After 24 h of incubation, CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs at the concentration of 0.063 g L−1 were added to the wells and incubated for 24 h in a CO2 incubator. Then, the samples were simultaneously stained with DAPI (blue fluorescent) in the amount of 1% of the volume of samples medium, respectively, for 15 min. The nanoparticles luminescence was detected in the green channel. Untreated cells were used as negative control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of oleate-oleylamine-stabilized and silica coated Cu(2−x)S NPs
ce:section.ce:para: It is worth noting that the solvothermal method is the most convenient one for getting oleate-oleylamine-stabilized Cu(2−x)S NPs with high size and phase uniformity. The initial plasmonic nanoparticles Cu(2−x)S were synthesized by means of the well-known hot-injection method with the use of oleylamine and oleic acid as stabilizers [14]. The TEM analysis indicates that the obtained copper sulfide nanoparticles are spherical with narrow size distribution. The average particles’ diameter was calculated to be about 8.71 nm (σ% = 7%, Fig. 1 A, B).

The XRD pattern of Cu(2−x)S NPs (Fig. 1C) has been compared with the XRD patterns of different forms of digenite available from the PXRD database (PDF-2). The comparison indicates that the XRD pattern of the prepared nanoparticles (Fig. 1C) fits the single-phase digenite (Сu1.765S) with crystallite sizes in the range of 8–16 nm (see Experimental Section, Suppl. Mat., Fig. S1). The localized surface plasmon resonance spectrum of the Cu(2−x)S NPs in cyclohexane is characterized by the band in NIR region centered at 1333 nm (Fig. 1D).

The Cu(2−x)S NPs were both doped and co-doped along with luminescent complex [Ru(dipy)3]Cl2 into SNs through the modified water-in-oil microemulsion procedure in order to reveal the interference of the hydrophobic and ionic dopants. The use of this procedure for the doping of Cu(2−x)S NPs was already documented [15,16], although specific redox and aggregation behavior of Cu(2−x)S NPs in the synthetic conditions should be minimized. The high redox activity of Cu(2−x)S NPs triggers the oxidative degradation of Cu(2−x)S NPs in the conditions of the ammonia-catalyzed hydrolysis of TEOS. Fig. 2 schematically illustrates the synthetic procedure, resulting in the safe doping of Cu(2−x)S NPs along with the embedding of amino-groups at the surface of the composite nanoparticles by the addition of APTES. The undesirable oxidation of the dopants was minimized by the continuous bubbling of argon current through the synthetic mixture.

It is worth noting the aggregation of Cu(2−x)S NPs in the synthetic mixture as the reason for the non-uniform inclusion of Cu(2−x)S NPs into silica nanobeads (Supp. Mat., Fig. S2), when the synthesis is facilitated by the magnetic stirring. The ultrasonication of the synthetic mixture facilitates the inclusion of the Cu(2−x)S NPs itself or along with [Ru(dipy)3]Cl2 into SNs with the size ranging from 36 to 65 nm, although the TEM image reveals also smaller sized nanoparticles ( Fig. 3a, b). The doped and co-doped hybrid nanoparticles which will be further designated as Cu(2−x)S@SNs and [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs correspondingly were manifested in the TEM images (Fig. 3) by the size ranging from 35 to 47 nm with the admixture of the single silica coated Cu(2−x)S NPs. The HR-TEM image of [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs confirms their composite nature derived from both inclusive and surface exposed localization of the Cu(2−x)S NPs within the composite nanomaterial.

The comparative size analysis revealed detectable size deviation between the oleate-oleylamine-coated Cu(2−x)S NPs (8.7 nm, Fig. 1) and silica coated Cu(2−x)S@SNs, where the size of Cu(2−x)S cores ranges within 5–7 nm (Fig. 3). The deviation can be explained by the release of the hydrophobic coating and partial degradation of Cu(2−x)S cores in the ammonia-rich synthetic conditions. The size and ratio of Cu(I)/Cu(II) ions at the interface of the Cu(2−x)S cores is of great impact on their spectral properties. The blue-shifting of the NIR emission band in Fig. 4 confirms the decrease in the size of the Cu(2−x)S cores under their embedding into silica spheres.

The Si:Cu and Si:Cu:Ru molar ratios of Cu(2−x)S@SNs and [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs ( Table 1) were calculated from the ICP-OES data in order to compare the efficacy of the inclusion of Cu(2−x)S cores into silica nanobeads. The analysis of Si:Cu ratios (Tables 1, S2) indicates that the copper content is greater when the synthesis is both facilitated by the ultrasonication and performed in the presence of [Ru(dipy)3]Cl2 (Table 1). Thus, the de-aggregation of oleate-oleylamine-stabilized Cu(2−x)S cores in the synthetic mixture is favored in the presence of [Ru(dipy)3]2+. In accordance with Si:Cu:Ru ratios (Table 1) [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs exhibit spectral properties derived from [Ru(dipy)3]2+, which is demonstrated by electronic absorption and emission spectra (Fig. 4). The concentration of the surface exposed amino-groups is 0.1–0.2 mM per 1 g∙L−1 of Cu(2−x)S@SNs or [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs (Fig. S3).

The well-known disadvantage of the amino-decorated SNs is their enhanced aggregation in neutral aqueous solutions due to the appearance of ammonium groups at the initially negatively charged silica surface [17–23]. The DLS measurements (Table 1) indicate that the aggregation of Cu(2−x)S@SNs is much greater than the previously reported aggregation behaviors of the amino-decorated silica nanobeads encapsulated by molecular or nanoparticulate dopants, such as the metal complexes or iron oxides [17–19,23]. It is worth assuming that the enhanced aggregation of Cu(2−x)S@SNs in great extent derives from both hydrophobicity and coordination ability of the surface exposed Cu(2−x)S cores.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Post-synthetic surface decoration of Cu(2−x)S@SNs and [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs
ce:section.ce:para: It is worth assuming that the colloid stability of [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs and Cu(2−x)S@SNs can be improved through the post-synthetic surface modification based on both coordinative binding and redox activity of the copper centers. However, an accessibility of the copper centers to coordinative binding, as well as an impact of the latter in a post-synthetic modification of the hybrid SNs are insufficiently recognized. The treating of the hybrid colloids by DL-histidine is followed by the decreased copper content (Table 1) resulted from the decreased size of the Cu(2−x)S-based cores ( Fig. 5a). The treating of Cu(2−x)S@SNs by PEI results in the whole dissolution of the cores and appearance of the dark exterior layer evident from the TEM images (Fig. 5b). The PEI-treated colloids are characterized by the increased positive ζ-values (Table 1) deriving from the copper-assisted self-assembly of PEI chains [17]. The localization of copper ions within the PEI-based layer is confirmed by the copper content in the PEI-treated colloids (Table 1), while the leached copper ions are revealed in the supernatant solutions after the treating of Cu(2−x)S@SNs by PEI and histidine (Fig. S4, Table S2).

The use of the citrate-stabilized carbon dots (CDs) in the surface modification of the composite SNs originates from their well-known affinity to copper ions [5]. Both green fluorescence of the CDs (Fig. 5c) and electrokinetic potential measurements (Table 1) make evident their deposition onto Cu(2−x)S@SNs and [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs after the post-treating procedure (Exp. Section). The ζ-values of the treated colloids designated as CDs-Cu(2−x)S@SNs (Table 1) reveal high negative surface charge, the latter is somewhat less negative for CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs due to presence of the surface exposed [Ru(dipy)3]2+ cations, although the aggregation behavior of both colloids is improved after the CD-treating (Table 1). The bands peculiar for the amide, carbonyl and alkyl groups arisen from the CDs are revealed through the comparative analysis of the FTIR spectra of CDs-Cu(2−x)S@SNs and the CDs (Fig. S5). The comparative analysis of the luminescence spectra of the CDs, CDs-Cu(2−x)S@SNs and CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs (Fig. 5c) indicates that the green fluorescence of the CDs in the hybrid colloids is red-shifted on 10–15 nm in comparison with the “free” CDs, while CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs exhibit dual green-red emission manifested by the two bands in the luminescence spectrum.

It is worth assuming that the adsorption of the citrate-stabilized CDs at the composite SNs is greatly contributed by the complex formation with the surface exposed copper centers. The assumption was confirmed by the additional measurements which indicate that the adsorption of the CDs is poor at the “empty” amino-modified SNs (Fig. S6), while their preliminary treating by Cu2+ (for more details see the Exp. Section) results in the efficient adsorption of the CDs. Moreover, the emission of the CDs exhibits the red-shifting under the adsorption onto both composite and treated by Cu2+ SNs (Fig. S6). This fact confirms the impact of the surface exposed Cu2+ ions on the adsorption of the CDs. Cartoon image (Fig. 5D) illustrates how the adsorption of the CDs onto [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs triggers their de-aggregation.

The ICP-OES analysis of the CD-treated colloids reveals significant decrease in the Si:Cu ratio (Table 1), which can be explained by the leaching of the copper ions resulted from their complexing with the CDs. However, the ICP-OES data (Table S3) reveal the silica content in both separated nanoparticulate and supernatant phases. This indicates that the CD-treating of the hybrid SNs restricts their centrifugation-induced phase separation. The ICP-OES data (Table S3) enable to evaluate the losses in the treated SNs. In particular, about half of CDs-Cu(2−x)S@SNs is revealed in the supernatant after the phase separation, while the separation of CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs is followed by the smaller losses (about 30%). The aforesaid difference agrees well with the less negative electrokinetic potential values of CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs versus CDs-Cu(2−x)S@SNs (Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization of chemodynamic activities of the composite SNs
ce:section.ce:para:
ESR technique facilitated by the use of the spin traps is a powerful tool in detection of ROS [20,23–25], including those generated by copper ions [26]. According to literature the ROS generation through the Cu2+-based Fenton like reaction requires weak acidic media and a presence of H2O2 [5,27]. However, the use of the spin trap 5,5-Dimethyl-1-Pyrroline-N-Oxide (DMPO) facilitated ESR technique revealed the spectral characteristics peculiar for DMPO∙OH∙ in aqueous solutions of Cu(2−x)S@SNs and [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs. High reductive potential of Cu+ is the reason for reduction of O2 according to the equilibrium (1) with further production of hydroxyl radicals through the equilibrium (2).
(1) 2Cu+ + O2 + 2H+ = 2Cu2+ + H2O2
(2) Cu+ + H2O2 + H+ = Cu2+ + OH∙ + H2O

The monitoring of the ESR spectra after the addition of the spin trap DMPO to aqueous colloids of both untreated and treated composite SNs indicates the radical species generation in the so-called dark conditions (Fig. S7, Fig. 6). However, the surface modification mode affects both spectral patterns of the spin adducts and their transformations in time. This, in turn, indicates that the generated radicals are of the different nature, which is the reason for the difference in their stability in time. In particular, the spectral pattern with the following parameters: g = 2.0055, aN = 14.8 G, aH = 14.8 G, ∆H = 0.7 G peculiar for DMPO-OH∙ spin adduct [28] is revealed for Cu(2−x)S@SNs and [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs (Fig. 6a). The signals of DMPO-OH∙ spin adducts remain at the similar level within one hour, which indicates stable in time generation of the hydroxyl radicals (Fig. S7). The SNs treated by both histidine and the CDs are characterized by two spectral patterns in the ESR spectra peculiar for DMPO-OH∙ and DMPO-X∙ spin adducts (Fig. 6b). The simulation of the spectral pattern of DMPO-X∙ in correlation with the reported patterns of the spin adducts derived from alkyl radicals [29] indicates that DMPO-X∙ derives from methyl and phenyl radicals (Fig. 6c–e). It is worth noting that for the treated SNs signal of DMPO-X∙ decreases within ~15 min, while that arisen from DMPO-OH∙ tends to remain unchanged (Fig. S7). However, the former signal is predominant over that of DMPO-OH∙ for the SNs treated by the CDs, which indicates the decreased ability of Cu(2−x)S cores to generate hydroxyl radicals in both CDs-Cu(2−x)S@SNs and CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs. This agrees well with the partial oxidative degradation of Cu(2−x)S cores and the following leaching of the copper ions from the SNs treated by the CDs.

The insignificant ROS generation by PEI-Cu(2−x)S@SNs and PEI-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs follows the similar tendency, which is in good correlation with the greatest oxidative degradation of Cu(2−x)S cores after their treating by PEI. It is also worth noting the greater impact of hydroxyl radicals versus alkyl and phenyl ones on generation of an oxidative stress in cells [30–32]. The results reveal the greater potential of the Cu(2−x)S@SNs and [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs in the ROS generation in comparison with their treated counterparts, which can be manifested by the different effect on the cell viability, although many other factors beyond the oxidative stress can affect the cytotoxicity of nanomaterial.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability and cell internalization of the composite SNs
ce:section.ce:para: It is worth noting the low cytotoxicity of both silica nanobeads itself and those doped with [Ru(dipy)3]-blocks ([Ru(dipy)3]@SNs) [19]. The incubation by [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs detectably decreases the cell viability of M-HeLa, WI-38 and Chang Liver cell lines, although the cytotoxic effect depends on both concentration of the SNs and the cell line nature, which is illustrated by Fig. 7A. In particular, the cell viability decrease is more pronounced for M-HeLa cells versus the normal cell lines exemplified by Chang Liver and WI-38. The enhanced cytotoxicity of [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs derives from the CDT of the surface exposed Cu(2−x)S cores, while the difference between the IC50 values measured for the cancer and normal cell lines indicates their potential in the cancer cell treating through the CDT. The flow cytometry data have been successfully performed only for the normal cell lines, since the cell internalization of [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs is detectable at the concentrations of the SNs greater than 0.2 g L−1, which is above the IC50 detected for M-HeLa cell line (Fig. 7C).

The fluorescent microscopy images of the Chang Liver cells incubated by [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs (0.25 g L−1) indicate the staining of the cytoplasm by the red emitting SNs, which becomes more pronounced at 0.35 g L−1 ( Fig. 8). The co-incubation by DAPI resulting in the staining of the cell nuclei by blue enables to assume the nuclear localization of some of the SNs.

The data collected in Table 2 enables to reveal the effect of the differently treated SNs on the IC50 values for M-HeLa and Chang Liver cell lines. The IC50 values represented in Table 2 refer to both concentration of the nanomaterial (g∙L−1) and the copper concentration (mMCu), since the latter is more convenient for comparison of the differently treated SNs. So, the cytotoxicity is somewhat increased after the treating of Cu(2−x)S@SNs by histidine, while the treating by PEI decreases the cytotoxic effect (Table 2). The IC50 values of [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs and Cu(2−x)S@SNs decrease after the treating by CDs (Table 2). The different IC50 values of the SNs treated by PEI, histidine and CDs (Table 2) highlight an impact of the oxidative conversion of Cu+→Cu2+ on the cytotoxic effect of the SNs.

The IC50 is greater for [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs than for Cu(2−x)S@SNs (Table 2). To explain the difference in their cytotoxicity it is worth noting the above mentioned assumption that [Ru(dipy)3]2+ facilitates the stripping of the oleates from Cu(2−x)S cores, which favors their inclusion into the SNs. Thus, the surface exposure of Cu(2−x)S-cores is less pronounced for [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs versus Cu(2−x)S@SNs Cu(2−x)S@SNs, which explains the difference in their cytotoxicity.

The greater cytotoxicity of CDs-Cu(2−x)S@SNs and CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs in comparison with the non-treated SNs (Table 2) points to some reason facilitating the cytotoxicity of the CD-treated SNs. Indeed, the flow cytometry data indicate more enhanced cell internalization of the CD-treated SNs versus the untreated ones (Fig. 7B). This tendency can be explained by the previously documented greater cell internalization of carbon dots versus inorganic nanoparticles [33]. Moreover, the fluorescent microscopy images with the additional staining of cell nuclei by DAPI reveal the efficient staining of both cytoplasm and nucleus of M-HeLa cells after their incubation by CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs (Fig. S8). The confocal microscopy images through both green and red channels confirm the nuclear localization of CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs ( Figs. 9, S9).

Taking into account the great importance of cell nuclei on the cell viability [34] the specific intracellular distribution manifested by the nuclear localization of CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs can enhance the oxidative stress of the cancer cells incubated by the SNs. The nuclear localization of nanoparticles depends on their size and surface modification, because the nuclear uptake of nanoparticles is greatly restricted by the structural features of the nuclear pore complexes (NPCs) [35,36]. In particular, the nanoparticulate size should be no more than 35–50 nm to entry into the nuclear compartment. The entrance can be greatly facilitated by a nuclear localization signal (NLS) which is represented by specific peptides characterized by short stretches of basic amino acids [37]. Moreover, the surface exposed amino/ammonium groups are the documented reason for the greater entrance into cell nuclei as it is illustrated by differently modified CDs. Indeed, the citrate-stabilized CDs tend to accumulate within cell cytoplasm [38], while those stabilized by cationic moieties demonstrated the nuclear localization [5,39]. It is worth noting that the size of [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs is within 35–47 nm (Table 1), which fit to the aforesaid size requirements of NPCs. However, both cell internalization and nuclear localization is rather poor for these SNs, which is in good correlation with their high aggregation behavior. The greater cell internalization and nuclear localization of CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs (Fig. 9) points to the poor aggregation as the factor facilitating their cell internalization, while their negative charge disagrees with the nuclear localization. The presented results are insufficient to highlight the factors responsible for such specific behavior. Nevertheless, it is worth assuming that high complex ability of the surface exposed copper ions can affect the interaction of CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs with the biorelevant macromolecules including proteins, which, in turn, can be a reason for the specific intracellular distribution.

Thus, the greater cytotoxicity of CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs versus [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs is due to the enhanced cell internalization followed by nuclear localization of the CDs-treated SNs, although the latter yield to [Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs in the ROS generation. Cell death resulted from the ROS-generation commonly undergoes through the apoptotic pathway which can be confirmed by means of the apoptosis assay. The apoptotic pathway of M-HeLa cells incubated by CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs was visualized through the ﬂow cytometry protocol with staining of apoptotic cells by annexin V- Alexa Fluor 647. The results represented in Fig. 10a clearly point to the apoptosis as the predominant mechanism of the cellular death under the incubation of M-HeLa cells by CDs-[Ru(dipy)3]Cu(2−x)S@SNs as it is schematically shown in Fig. 10b.

The present work represents synthetic and post-synthetic modes to vary both size and surface exposure of Cu(2−x)S cores co-doped with red emitting [Ru(dipy)3]2+ into amino-modified silica nanoparticles. The synthetic conditions were optimized for the de-aggregation of Cu(2−x)S cores as the prerequisite for their efficient embedding along with [Ru(dipy)3]2+ into the amino-modified SNs. The spin trapped ESR technique revealed efficient ROS-generation in the oxygenated aqueous solutions by the composite SNs. The enhanced cytotoxicity of the composite SNs versus the “empty” SNs reveals the former as promising candidates for CDT. However, the enhanced aggregation of the composite SNs restricts their cell internalization, in turn, limiting the cell treating.

The coordinative binding of PEI and citrate-stabilized CDs with the interfacial copper ions both provides their adsorption onto the SNs and the oxidative leaching of the Cu(2−x)S cores. The adsorption of PEI and the CDs results in positive and negative recharging of the post-treated SNs, which is followed by their de-aggregation. The ability of the composite SNs to generate ROS comes to zero under the post-treating by PEI due to the great extent of the oxidative leaching, while the ROS-generation is somewhat decreased but still detectable for the SNs treated by the CDs. The green or dual green-red emission of the CD-treated composite SNs enables to visualize their cell internalization and intracellular localization by means of fluorescence and confocal microscopy methods. Correlation of cytotoxicity data of the differently treated composite SNs with their ability to ROS generation and the intracellular localization highlight the enhanced cell internalization and nuclear confinement of the CD-treated composite SNs as the factor beyond the ROS-generation enhancing their cytotoxicity through the apoptotic mechanism.

Svetlana Fedorenko: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Alexey Stepanov: Resources, Investigation. Olga Bochkova: Resources, Investigation. Kirill Kholin: Formal analysis. Alexey Dovjenko: Resources, Investigation. Rustem Zairov: Resources, Investigation. Irek Nizameev: Formal analysis. Tatiana Gerasimova: Formal analysis. Igor Strelnik: Resources, Investigation. Alexandra Voloshina: Formal analysis, Investigation. Anastasia Sapunova: Formal analysis, Investigation. Syumbelya Gumerova: Formal analysis. Aidar Gubaidullin: Formal analysis. Svetlana Fedosimova: Formal analysis. Vladimir Evtugyn: Formal analysis. Asiya Toropchina: Formal analysis. Andrey Karasik: Formal analysis. Asiya Mustafina: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors gratefully acknowledge to Assigned Spectral-Analytical Center of FRC Kazan Scientific Center of RAS for providing necessary facilities to carry out physical-chemical measurements. The authors thanks to Interdisciplinary Center for Analytical Microscopy at KFU for assistance with the confocal microscopy experiments. The work was supported by RFBR and CNR Project No. 20-53-7802.

ce:section.ce:para: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).
